source_metadata.title_raw: Loren Soukup - I-19-089 - June 5, 2019 - Santa Rosa

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Loren Soukup 
Senior Associate General Counsel 
5350 Skylane Boulevard 
Santa Rosa, CA 95403 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. 1-19-089 
Dear Ms. Soukup: 
June 5, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Because your question is general in nature, we are treating 
your request as one for informal assistance. 2 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTIONS 
1. 
May Trustee Barrow take part in negotiations between the Sausalito-Marin City School 
District Board of Directors ("the Board") and the Attorney General's Office ("AG") that 
may result in numerous changes to a school within 500 feet of his property, including 
facility changes, construction, and increased enrollment? 
. 2. 
May Trustee Barrow take part in the District Board of Director's approval of a "2018-2019 
Second Interim Budget," despite his real property economic interest? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3).) 


CONCLUSIONS 
File No. 1-19-089 
Page No. 2 
1. 
Whether Trustee Barrow may take part in a governmental decision is a question determined 
on a case-by-case basis. With respect to any decision that involves a reasonably foreseeable 
financial effect on his real property economic interest, he will presumably be prohibited 
from taking part in the decision. However, without sufficient details regarding any specific 
governmental decision, we cannot feasibly determine whether he is prohibited from taking 
part in the decision. 
2. 
Because the 2018-2019 Second Interim Budget would not have any reasonably foreseeable 
financial effect on Trustee Barrow's real property interests, he may take part in the Board's 
decision to approve or deny the budget. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
Joshua Barrow is an elected member of the Sausalito-Marin City School District Board of 
Trustees. Trustee Barrow and his spouse own residential real property in Marin City, along with a 
view easement over "Lot 2" of the adjacent property, which the Barrows are in the process of 
purchasing. Lot 2 is part of a larger parcel owned by the Marin City Church of God, which is 
retaining ownership of "Lot 1." 
The only public school operated by the District is Bayside-Martin Luther King Jr. Academy 
("Bayside"). Approximately 125 students attend Bayside. Trustee Barrow's residential real property 
is located approximately 235 feet from the nearest property line at Bayside School. The edge of Lot 
2 is approximately 80 feet from the edge of the Bayside property. 
Trustee Barrow is seeking advice on whether he may take part in negotiations with the AG, 
as well as the potential planning process that may come out of the negotiations, given the proximity 
of his residence to Bayside. The AG has advised the Board that it believes that the District has 
violated anti-discrimination laws with respect to its establishment and operation of Bayside. In 
response, the Board is considering enacting changes to the education program at Bayside. The 
Board may also negotiate with the AG and vote on any resolution regarding the AG's concerns. 
You do not currently know the specific terms that may come out of the Board's negotiations with 
the AG, but you do know that the negotiations may result in changes to the District's programs, 
facility changes or construction, enrollment, grade levels served, school hours, and staffing. You 
believe that an increase in enrollment would not be greater than an additional 100 students over a 
five to ten-year period. Any increase in traffic attributable to that increase would likewise be 
gradual and would only occur two times per day ( during drop off and pick up), and you believe it 
would be unlikely to have any noticeable effect on Trustee Barrow's street, or the adjoining street. 
In a follow-up phone call with Trustee Barrow, he stated that he lives on a court, so his 
street does not experience through-traffic and, to his knowledge, his family are the only residents on 
that court with school age children. He also stated that any newly constructed building would have 
to be approximately six stories tall to alter the view from his elevated property, and, to his 
knowledge, no such construction is being considered, given that the rest of the buildings on the 
school property are approximately one to two stories. Rather, construction presumably being 
considered includes improvements such as replacing outdated portables, upgrading a ball field, 


File No. 1-19-089 
Page No. 3 
tearing down a seismically unstable facility currently used for storage ("the Annex"), and creating 
additional spaces to accommodate a potential increase in the number of enrolled students. In 
another follow-up phone call, Trustee Barrow explained that he is unaware of the scope of the terms 
being considered during the negotiation process, because he has thus far sat out of the negotiation 
process entirely. 
Because of the proximity of Trustee Barrow's real property to the Bayside school grounds, 
he is also seeking advice regarding his ability to review and approve (or deny) the "2018-2019 
Second Interim Budget" for the District. The Board will be holding a public hearing on the budget 
on June 13th and final action will take place on June 20th. You provided an agenda packet from a 
March 14, 2019 meeting involving the budget. No funds from the budget were allotted for "land" or 
"land improvement." Those categories are listed in the budget regardless of whether funds ·are 
allotted for them. Expenditure categories with allotted funds include "classified salaries," 
"employee benefits," "books and supplies," and "services and other operating expenditures," which 
includes "travel and conferences," as well as "operations and housekeeping services," among other 
expenses. It is anticipated that the budget will remain nearly identical once presented at the June 
2019 meetings. 
ANALYSIS 
Under Section 87100, "[ n ]o public official at any level of state or local government shall 
make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a 
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest." A 
public official makes a governmental decision if the official authorizes or directs any action, votes, 
appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into a 
contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18704(a).) A public official 
participates in a governmental decision if the official provides information, an opinion, or a 
recommendation for the purpose of affecting the decision without significant intervening 
substantive review. (Regulation 18704(b ). ) 
Interests 
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if 
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable 
from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or 
on an enumerated interest. (Section 87103.) Such interests include "[a]ny real property in which the 
public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more." 
(Section 87103(b).) Here, Trustee Barrow's real property is presumably worth well over $2,000, 
and thus constitutes an economic interest. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if 
the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent 
extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably 
foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).) 


File No. 1-19-089 
Page No. 4 
Under Regulation 18702.2(a)(7), the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a parcel of real property in which an official has a financial interest, other 
than a leasehold interest, is material whenever the governmental decision involves ,property located 
500 feet or less from the property line of the parcel, unless there is clear and convincing evidence 
that the decision will not have any measurable impact on the official's property. Examples of 
factors considered in determining whether there is a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on real 
property include if the decision would change the property's character by substantially altering 
traffic levels, intensity of use, parking, view, privacy, noise levels, air quality, or market value. 
(Regulation 18702.2(8)(D)-(E).) 
Segmentation 
Under Regulation 18706(a), an agency may segment a decision in which a public official 
has a financial interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the following 
conditions apply: 
1. The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken down into separate 
decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decision in which the official has a 
disqualifying financial interest; 
2. The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented from the other 
decisions; 
3. The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first and a final 
decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified official's participation in any 
way; and 
4. Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been made, the 
disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening·of, or otherwise 
financially affect, the decision from which the official was disqualified. 
Decisions are "inextricably interrelated," within the meaning of Regulation 18706(a), when the 
result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another 
decision. (Regulation 18706(b ). ) 
Negotiations with the AG 
In your request for advice, you indicated that the negotiations may result in changes to the 
District's programs, facility changes or construction, enrollment, grade levels served, school hours, 
and staffing. However, you did not identify any specific governmental decision(s) that would be 
involved in the negotiation process. 
Without sufficient details pertaining to a specific governmental decision, it is not feasible to 
determine the extent to which Trustee Barrow may be disqualified from taking part in the 
negotiation process with the AG. It is reasonably foreseeable that governmental decisions involving 
construction on Bayside property may have a financial effect on Trustee Barrow's real property 
interests. Even if construction does not alter the view from a particular property, it may, for 
instance, affect noise and traffic levels, as well as air quality. Because Trustee Barrow's property is 
located within 500 feet of the Bayside property where construction may occur as a result of 


File No. 1-19-089 
Page No. 5 
governmental decisions made during the negotiation process, he would presumably be disqualified 
from taking part in the negotiation process. However, if Trustee Barrow is able to provide sufficient 
facts related to a specific governmental decision, he may be able to establish clear and convincing 
evidence that the decision would not have a material effect on his real property interest, or we may 
find that the "public generally" exception is applicable. (See Regulation 18703.) 
Thus far, Trustee Barrow has not taken part in the negotiation process at all, such that he is 
unaware of any specific terms being discussed or considered. Trustee Barrow will need to identify a 
specific governmental decision implicating his economic interests, before we can determine 
whether he is permitted or disqualified from taking part in the negotiation process. He may want to 
discuss the status of the AG negotiations with the Board's General Counsel, in order to better 
understand the scope of the negotiations and identify which specific governmental decisions, if any, 
he would potentially be prohibited from taking part in. 
We also emphasize that the determination of whether a public official may take part in a 
governmental decision is made on a case-by-case basis. The negotiation process may ultimately 
involve numerous governmental decisions, some of which Trustee Barrow would be permitted to 
take part in if properly segmented from the decisions in which he is disqualified from taking part in. 
(See Regulation 18706.) For instance, it may be the case that certain decisions, such as 
implementing new school programs or hiring additional teachers, or a new Superintendent, would 
not have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on Trustee Barrow's real property 
interests. If properly segmented from the decisions he is disqualified from, Trustee Barrow would 
still be able to take part in certain aspects of the negotiation process. 
Final Approval of the 2018-2019 Second Interim Budget 
As noted, Trustee Barrow has an economic interest in his real property, which is located less 
than 500 feet from Bayside. However, based on the description of the agenda packet you provided, 
it does not appear that the decision to approve or deny the proposed budget would have a 
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on Trustee Barrow's property as none of the budgeted 
expenditures are property related or likely to result in a significant effect on Bayside's property or 
adjacent properties. Although it is possible that the money allotted to specific categories of 
expenditures could lead to overall improvements at Bayside in educational quality, any potential 
financial effect from the budget itself on adjacent properties would be merely hypothetical or 
theoretical at this time. (See Regulation 18702(b).) Accordingly, Trustee Barrow is not disqualified 
from taking part in the Board's decision to approve or deny the 2018-2019 Second Interim Budget. 
We recommend that Trustee Barrow review the conflict of interest materials available on 
our website. If he has questions regarding a specific governmental decision, and is able to provide 
sufficient details regarding that decision, he should seek additional advice from the Commission. 


File No. 1-19-089 
Page No. 6 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
KMC:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Kevin Cornwall 
Counsel, Legal Division
